docs.title: Rebuilding Computer Science From Scratch
docs.text:
Learn computer science the way humanity could have invented it.

Not by memorizing algorithms.

Not by copying tutorials.

By rebuilding every invention from first principles.

Every discovery begins with a problem.

Every discovery ends with working code.

docs.title: Start
docs.text: • Read: Discovery 01 — Why Counting Exists

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text:
• [x] Why Counting Exists
  • [ ] Why Numbers Need Memory
  • [ ] Why Arrays Exist
  • [ ] Why Memory Has Addresses
  • [ ] Why Copying Is Expensive
  • [ ] Why Locality Matters
  • [ ] Why Looking Is Slow
  • [ ] Why Ordering Helps
  • [ ] Why Binary Search Exists
  • [ ] Why Trees Exist
  • [ ] Why Balancing Exists
  • [ ] Why Hashing Exists

See the full roadmap

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: You can compare piles of rocks, but you cannot say how many.

docs.title: Challenge
docs.text: Implement the smallest program that demonstrates 'Counting' before reading the solution.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text: Write a minimal implementation of 'Counting', then measure where it breaks.

docs.title: Engineering Notes
docs.text:
This discovery exists because the previous approach failed under a real constraint. The lesson is not 'Counting' as a fact, but as a response to pressure.

docs.text: Implement the smallest program that demonstrates 'KV Cache' before reading the solution.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text: Write a minimal implementation of 'KV Cache', then measure where it breaks.

docs.title: Engineering Notes
docs.text:
This discovery exists because the previous approach failed under a real constraint. The lesson is not 'KV Cache' as a fact, but as a response to pressure.

Continue → Why Quantization Exists

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: Models are too large to fit on consumer hardware.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: One GPU cannot hold a model, but many can if the work is split.

docs.text: Every token activates the entire model, wasting computation.

Continue → Why Inference Servers Exist

docs.text: Thousands of users ask for completions at the same time.

Continue → Projects

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
• Discover before defining.
  • Build before abstracting.
  • No black boxes.
  • Every chapter ends with code.

docs.title: Rule 1
docs.text:
Every discovery begins with reality.

Never start with an algorithm.

Start with a problem.

docs.title: Rule 2
docs.text:
The learner should suffer the problem before seeing the solution.

Never introduce Hash Maps.

Instead ask:

Why is searching becoming slow?

docs.title: Rule 3
docs.text: Every discovery introduces exactly one new idea.

docs.title: Rule 4
docs.text:
Implement everything once.

Arrays. Linked Lists. Hash Tables. Heaps. Trees. Schedulers. Caches. Consensus.

Everything.

docs.title: Rule 5
docs.text:
Every abstraction must already have a concrete implementation.

Libraries come last.

docs.title: Rule 6
docs.text:
Every discovery must answer:

Why couldn't the previous solution continue?
